plainMeaning: Reverence to the awakened ones who not only won the inner battle, crossed the ocean of rebirth, woke up, and freed themselves — but also help everyone else do the very same.
recitation: jiṇāṇaṁ jāvayāṇaṁ, tinnāṇaṁ tārayāṇaṁ, buddhāṇaṁ bohayāṇaṁ, muttāṇaṁ moagāṇaṁ

wordByWord.gloss: to the conquerors (the Jinas) — those who have vanquished the inner foes
wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of Prakrit jiṇa < Sanskrit jina, from √ji 'to conquer'; here genitive in the salutation sense, 'to/of'.
wordByWord.notes: The reflexive (self-directed) member of the first couplet: one who has won the victory in himself.

wordByWord.gloss: to the enablers-of-conquest — those who make others conquer
wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of jāvaya < Sanskrit jāpaka, causative of √ji ('to cause to conquer'); Prakrit -p- > -v-.
wordByWord.notes: The transitive (other-directed) member: not merely a victor, but one who confers victory on others. Some editions read jāpayāṇaṁ; sense unchanged.

wordByWord.gloss: to those who have crossed over (the ocean of saṁsāra)
wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of tinna < Sanskrit tīrṇa, past participle of √tṝ 'to cross'.
wordByWord.notes: Reflexive member of the second couplet.

wordByWord.gloss: to the ferriers — those who carry others across
wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of tāraya < Sanskrit tāraka, agent-noun from causative √tṝ ('one who causes to cross').
wordByWord.notes: Transitive member; the very root of tīrtha ('ford') and tīrthaṅkara ('ford-maker').

wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of buddha, past participle of √budh 'to awaken, to know'.
wordByWord.notes: Reflexive member of the third couplet; here in its Jain sense (an awakened Jina), not the Buddhist title.

wordByWord.gloss: to the awakeners — those who awaken others
wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of bohaya < Sanskrit bodhaka, agent-noun from causative √budh ('one who causes awakening'); cf. bodhi (enlightenment).
wordByWord.notes: Transitive member; the Arihanta as giver of bodhi to others.

wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of mutta < Sanskrit mukta, past participle of √muc 'to release'.
wordByWord.notes: Reflexive member of the fourth couplet.

wordByWord.gloss: to the liberators — those who set others free
wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of moaga < Sanskrit mocaka, agent-noun from causative √muc ('one who causes release'); Prakrit -c- elided, -aga from -aka.
wordByWord.notes: Transitive member, closing the four-couplet sequence.

literalTranslation: To the conquerors and the enablers-of-conquest; to the crossed-over and the ferriers; to the awakened and the awakeners; to the freed and the liberators.
idiomaticTranslation: Reverence be to the Victors who make others victorious; to those who have crossed the flood and ferry others across; to the Awakened who awaken the world; to the Liberated who set all beings free.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This single clause is the devotional heart of the whole stava, built as four tight couplets in which each epithet is voiced twice — once reflexively (what the Arihanta achieved in himself) and once transitively (what he bestows on others): jiṇa→jāvaya, tinna→tāraya, buddha→bohaya, mutta→moaga. The grammar carries the theology. The first word of each pair is a past participle — a finished state (he has conquered, crossed, awakened, been freed); the second is a causative agent-noun in -aya/-aga — an ongoing compassionate activity (he makes-conquer, ferries, awakens, liberates). Placed immediately after the preceding verse's claim that the Arihanta has cast off the veil of chadma and possesses unobstructed kevala knowledge-and-vision, this verse answers the natural question 'and what does such a perfected one do?' — he turns outward. Note the deliberate ordering, which recapitulates the spiritual path itself: victory over the passions (jiṇa) precedes crossing saṁsāra (tinna), which yields awakening (buddha), which culminates in final release (mutta). The four reflexive terms thus trace the arc from first conquest to final liberation; the four transitive terms show that arc reopened for every other being. The verse then hands off to the next, where the freed-and-freeing one is named all-knowing, all-seeing, and arrived at the deathless abode.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The couplets encode the precise Jain distinction between a Sāmānya-kevalin (an ordinary omniscient who is jiṇa, tinna, buddha, mutta for himself alone) and a Tīrthaṅkara (who is additionally jāvaya, tāraya, bohaya, moaga). The second member of each pair is the working-out of tīrthaṅkara-nāma-karma — the merit-stock that compels a Jina to found the four-fold tīrtha and teach. Crucially, 'liberator' (moaga) is not to be read as a grace-giver who hands out mokṣa: in Jain doctrine each soul liberates itself by its own effort. The Arihanta is moaga only as cause-by-instruction — he shows the ford (tāraya) and awakens insight (bohaya); the crossing is the disciple's own. The verse therefore holds two truths in balance: the soul's self-reliant agency and the indispensable role of the awakened teacher.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When a reciter reaches these eight words, the salutation quietly becomes a self-examination and an aspiration: having bowed to one who both attained and shared, the practitioner is invited to receive (let oneself be ferried, awakened, freed) and, in due measure, to give — to carry, teach, and free others rather than hoard one's own progress. It reframes spiritual effort as never merely private.

crossReferences.text: Caityavandana / Praṇipāta-stava (Namutthuṇaṁ) as recited at the opening of every caityavandana

sources.title: The Aupapâtika Sûtra (Uvavāiya), ed. & tr. Ernst Leumann
sources.consultedFor: Canonical attestation of the Namutthuṇaṁ couplets jiṇāṇaṁ jāvayāṇaṁ … muttāṇaṁ moagāṇaṁ as the āgama formula of homage to the Arihantas.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses and Sanskrit equivalents of jāvaya (jāpaka), tāraya (tāraka), bohaya (bodhaka), moaga (mocaka) and their causative formation.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Verification of the underlying Sanskrit roots and causative agent-nouns: √ji/jāpaka, √tṝ/tāraka, √budh/bodhaka, √muc/mocaka.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim Gujarati-script verse text and its placement as clause 8 of the Śakra-stava within the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.

notes: The eight tokens give 100% coverage; all are genitive plurals used in the salutation ('to the …') sense governed by the sutra's opening namo. The reflexive/transitive pairing is well established and matches the preface; the chief interpretive choice is reading the second member of each couplet as a true causative agent-noun (jāpaka/tāraka/bodhaka/mocaka) rather than a synonym — supported by Sheth's lexicon and the canonical text. Minor orthographic/variant readings exist across editions (e.g. jāvayāṇaṁ vs jāpayāṇaṁ; spacing of moaga), with no change of sense; Gujarati-script forms here follow the book verbatim. The doctrinal caveat that moaga is liberator-by-instruction, not a bestower of mokṣa, reflects standard Jain self-effort doctrine and is not in dispute.
